nodes.label: P07_T001
nodes.identity_summary: A price-conscious student who prioritizes solving dry skin problems through research-backed products, relying heavily on friend recommendations and detailed online reviews while maintaining convenience through quick commerce. Despite being open to trying new brands, they require substantial validation before switching from products that work for their specific skin concerns.

nodes.label: dry-skin-problem-solver-first
nodes.description: Consistently prioritizes moisturizing and hydrating properties above all other product benefits due to ongoing dry skin issues.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Moisturizing and hydration are inadequate—skin feels dry after use. Has granular texture that exfoliates roughly, making skin rough post-bath. Need to apply moisturizer afterward.
[REAL] Was using Nivea; switched to Dove because Nivea left dry patches and dried skin, requiring heavy moisturizer. Dove solved the dryness partially but not fully.
[SYNTHETIC] A) Stop using it immediately and go back to your old body wash. Dry skin is already my main concern, so anything making it worse needs to be discontinued immediately.
[SYNTHETIC] The ₹429 one I just sampled — the trial convinced me. If it solves my dryness problem better than my regular one, the extra cost is worth it.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new body wash concept, this person would likely focus first on moisturizing claims and ingredients that address dryness, dismissing products that don't explicitly solve this problem.

nodes.label: friend-family-recommendation-prioritizer
nodes.description: Places highest trust in personal recommendations from friends and family, rating them 5/5 compared to other information sources.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you trust the following sources for personal care product information? Friends and family: 5
[SYNTHETIC] My close friend's recommendation. I trust friends and family most for personal care advice, much more than any social media content.
[SYNTHETIC] C) Your mother's recommendation: 5 points, D) Amazon India reviews: 3 points, B) Reddit thread: 2 points
[SYNTHETIC] B) I'd trust my friend more — they know my preferences better.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown marketing content, this person would likely dismiss it in favor of asking friends for their actual experiences with the product, making peer testimonials more effective than celebrity endorsements.

nodes.label: price-conscious-student-budget-optimizer
nodes.description: Consistently prioritizes price as the top purchase factor, spending in the ₹200-350 range and actively seeking value propositions.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When buying any personal care product, rank these factors from most to least important: price, brand, recommendation
[REAL] How much do you typically spend on a single body wash purchase? 200_350
[SYNTHETIC] Price/deal: 35 points, Past experience: 30 points, Specific skin benefit: 20 points
[SYNTHETIC] Made in India at ₹249. I have no preference between Indian and international brands - I go with value proposition. Same product at ₹200 less is an easy choice.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown premium-positioned products, this person would likely immediately check the price and seek cheaper alternatives that deliver similar benefits rather than paying for brand prestige.

nodes.label: quick-commerce-dependency-for-convenience
nodes.description: Relies on quick commerce as first instinct when needing body wash, prioritizing 15-minute delivery over selection variety.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Yes, limited selection definitely impacts purchases. When out of body wash or personal care products, my first instinct is to open Blinkit. If the product isn't available there, 99% chance I won't buy it.
[SYNTHETIC] Buy from Blinkit at ₹299. When I'm almost out of body wash, my first instinct is to open Blinkit. The convenience matters more than saving ₹100 when I need it immediately.
[SYNTHETIC] Order from Blinkit and get whatever's available in 15 minutes. When I'm out of body wash, I need it immediately.
[SYNTHETIC] Quick commerce (Blinkit/Zepto). It's already my first instinct when I run out of body wash, and the convenience of 15-minute delivery outweighs the limited selection.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new product concept, this person would likely only consider it if it's available on quick commerce platforms, dismissing brands that require special ordering or aren't in their immediate delivery ecosystem.

nodes.label: research-validation-before-new-purchases
nodes.description: Systematically researches products through multiple sources, particularly Reddit and Amazon reviews, before trying anything new.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you read online reviews before buying personal care products? Where? Reddit, amazon, nykaa, google
[SYNTHETIC] C) Research the claim yourself before deciding. I don't trust social media content blindly - I'd want to verify the greenwashing allegations myself.

[SYNTHETIC] A) Order immediately — community recommendations are gold. Reddit users provide honest reviews without brand bias. If 8/10 people love it, that's stronger validation than celebrity endorsements.
[SYNTHETIC] After it gets listed on Amazon/Nykaa with 100+ reviews. I need substantial user feedback before trying new D2C brands, especially for products that go on my skin.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new brand launch, this person would likely wait for substantial online reviews to accumulate before purchasing, making early-stage marketing less effective than building authentic review volume.

nodes.label: anti-social-media-marketing-skeptic
nodes.description: Actively distrusts social media influencers and advertisements, rating them 1/5 for trustworthiness in personal care decisions.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you trust the following sources for personal care product information? TV advertisements: 1, Instagram or YouTube influencers: 1
[REAL] Never bought a product from social media. Don't trust the platform for personal care—too many fake influencers and fake product promotions on Instagram.
[SYNTHETIC] Neither influences me much since I don't trust influencers for personal care. I'd research both products myself on Reddit and Amazon reviews before deciding.
[SYNTHETIC] (d) Ignore and continue scrolling. I don't trust social media for personal care purchases.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown influencer-driven campaigns or social media ads, this person would likely actively avoid the promoted products, making traditional digital marketing counterproductive for reaching them.

nodes.label: comfortable-new-brand-experimenter
nodes.description: Rates comfort with trying new brands as 4/5, actively switching between different brands like Nivea, Dove, Man Matters, and Minimalist.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How comfortable are you trying new brands or products you haven't used before? 4
[REAL] What body wash brand(s) do you currently use? List all. Nivea, Dove, Man Matters, Minimalist

[SYNTHETIC] I'd pick Brand B at ₹199. Better ingredients at a lower price is exactly the value proposition I look for. I'm comfortable trying new brands, and the ingredient list matters more than brand recognition for me.
[SYNTHETIC] C) Different brands each time. I've switched between Nivea, Dove, Man Matters, and Minimalist recently.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new brand with clear value proposition, this person would likely be willing to try it immediately if the price and benefits align, making them receptive to challenger brands over established ones.

nodes.label: moderate-brand-loyalty-with-switching-triggers
nodes.description: Shows moderate loyalty to brands but will switch for better smell, skin problems, or friend recommendations.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How loyal are you to personal care brands? Moderate_loyalty
[REAL] What would make you switch away from your current body wash? Pick your top 3: better_smell, skin_problem, friend_rec
[REAL] If a brand you liked raised its price by 30%, what would you do? Look_alternatives
[SYNTHETIC] B) Try the D2C brand your friend recommended. ₹80 increase is significant, and friend recommendations carry high trust value for me.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown competitive products, this person would likely switch if the new option offers better fragrance, solves skin issues more effectively, or comes recommended by trusted sources, making loyalty-based retention strategies less effective.

nodes.label: oceanic-fresh-fragrance-preference
nodes.description: Specifically gravitates toward ocean/aquatic fragrances and wants energizing, wake-me-up scents that last half a day.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What fragrance families do you naturally gravitate toward? Pick your top 2: Ocean_aquatic, woody_musky
[REAL] Love a perfume with fresh oceanic fragrance—it lasted in my mind long. Haven't repurchased yet (not finished), but definitely will. Special because it gave a fresh oceanic vibe.
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch: Fragrance: fresh oceanic, energizing/wake-me-up
[REAL] Combine my oceanic perfume fragrance with my body wash texture—a subtle, not overpowering oceanic scent in a body wash would be ideal.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown fragrance options, this person would likely be immediately drawn to ocean/aquatic scents and dismiss floral or heavy fragrances, making scent positioning critical for product appeal.

nodes.label: clinical-evidence-over-traditional-claims
nodes.description: Prefers clinically proven products over Ayurvedic or traditional wisdom, trusting scientific evidence and dermatologist recommendations.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Would you prefer a product that is 'clinically proven' or one that is 'inspired by traditional/Ayurvedic wisdom'? Slight_clinical
[REAL] How much do you trust the following sources for personal care product information? Dermatologists or skin doctors: 4
[SYNTHETIC] The clinical claims matter more. While fermented ingredients sound interesting, I prefer products with proven effectiveness for my skin concerns over traditional wisdom alone.
[SYNTHETIC] B) The brand - they have actual test data. Scientific evidence and test reports are more credible than individual opinions, even from experts.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown traditional vs. clinical positioning, this person would likely choose the clinically-backed option and respond well to dermatologist endorsements while dismissing Ayurvedic heritage claims.

nodes.label: essential-lather-texture-requirements
nodes.description: Considers lather and foam essential to the body wash experience, preferring lightweight textures with good foaming properties.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How important is lather/foam to your body wash experience? Essential
[REAL] What texture do you prefer in a body wash? Lightweight
[REAL] What is your ideal post-wash skin feeling? Moisturized
[SYNTHETIC] 2, 4, 1, 3 - Effectiveness and travel size matter most for gym use.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown product demonstrations, this person would likely focus on lather quality and texture feel, dismissing products that don't foam well or feel too heavy on the skin.

nodes.label: hygiene-over-convenience-prioritizer
nodes.description: Exclusively uses body wash over bar soap for hygiene reasons, never compromising on this preference.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you currently use body wash, bar soap, or both? What drives that choice? Body wash—more hygienic than bar soap.
[REAL] Do you ever use bar soap, or is it exclusively body wash for you? Exclusively body wash.
[SYNTHETIC] (a) Order on Zepto, (c) Borrow from flatmate, (d) Buy during lunch break, (b) Use bar soap. I exclusively use body wash, never bar soap.
[SYNTHETIC] B) Order a body wash on Blinkit/Zepto for quick delivery. I exclusively use body wash and find it more hygienic than bar soap.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown soap alternatives or hybrid products, this person would likely reject them entirely, making body wash format non-negotiable and soap-to-body-wash conversion messaging irrelevant.

nodes.label: values-driven-but-not-premium-paying
nodes.description: Describes self as values-driven but unwilling to pay premiums for sustainability, cruelty-free, or ethical claims.

[REAL] What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? Sustainable/eco-friendly packaging: 0, Ayurvedic or traditional ingredients: 0, Clinical-grade, lab-tested formulation: 0, Cruelty-free, not tested on animals: 0, Made in India: 0
[SYNTHETIC] C) ₹0 — these claims don't influence what I'm willing to pay. I'm not willing to pay premium for sustainability or social impact.
[SYNTHETIC] A) Stick with my current brand — the improved formula is what matters, and I separate products from politics.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown values-based marketing or premium ethical positioning, this person would likely ignore these claims entirely and focus purely on functional benefits and price, making cause marketing ineffective.

nodes.label: packaging-indifferent-function-focused

[SYNTHETIC] B) Skip it - I'm paying for the product, not the bottle. ₹150 extra for just packaging doesn't add any value to my routine.
[SYNTHETIC] B) Skip it — I'm paying for the formula, not the packaging.

nodes.description: Considers product availability in their shopping locations as a practical barrier to purchase.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Might not be available where I shop
[SURVEY] What concerns would you have about trying the Dove Skin ID? Might not be available where I shop
nodes.predictive_value: When considering new products, this person would likely factor in distribution and availability before making purchase decisions.

nodes.label: gentle-skin-treatment-requirer
nodes.description: Values gentleness on skin as an important factor, likely due to their dry skin concerns.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Gentleness on skin: 4
[SURVEY] dry skin problems
[SURVEY] moisturization actually lasts 12 hours on dry skin types
nodes.predictive_value: When shown products with harsh ingredients or strong formulations, this person would likely be concerned about skin irritation and prefer gentler alternatives.

nodes.label: fragrance-quality-appreciator
nodes.description: Values good fragrance and scent quality as an important but secondary consideration after skin benefits.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Fragrance / scent quality: 4
[SURVEY] The personalized fragrance technology sounds innovative, but it's not relevant
nodes.predictive_value: When evaluating products with similar skin benefits, this person would likely use fragrance quality as a differentiating factor in their final choice.

nodes.label: no-concerns-when-needs-align
nodes.description: Shows no hesitation about trying products when they directly address their specific needs.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns would you have about trying the Dove Skip? No concerns
[SURVEY] Based on what you've seen, how likely would you be to buy the Dove Skip? Definitely would buy
[SURVEY] How relevant is the Dove Skip to your personal needs? Extremely relevant
nodes.predictive_value: When shown products that perfectly match their dry skin needs, this person would likely move quickly to purchase without typical new product hesitations.

links.mechanism: The ongoing dry skin issues make them specifically interested in formulations that address hard water effects on skin, as they recognize water quality significantly impacts their skin condition.
While they highly trust friend recommendations, they still seek additional validation from multiple sources, creating tension between personal trust and systematic research.
Limited student budget makes them risk-averse to expensive full-size purchases, driving preference for smaller trial sizes to test before committing larger amounts.
The habit of using quick commerce as first instinct reinforces their willingness to prioritize immediate availability over price savings or selection variety.
Distrust of social media marketing drives them to seek authentic validation through multiple research sources like Reddit and Amazon reviews before making decisions.
Comfort with trying new brands enables their moderate loyalty approach, making them willing to switch when better options present themselves.
Preference for clinical evidence drives desire to understand ingredients and formulations, making transparency more valuable than traditional or heritage claims.
Being values-driven but unwilling to pay premiums reinforces focus on functional effectiveness rather than brand positioning or ethical claims.
Indifference to packaging aesthetics reinforces their broader pattern of prioritizing functional benefits over experiential or aesthetic features.
Since fragrance is essential, they've developed specific preferences for oceanic scents that align with their desire to smell fresh and avoid smelling bad.
Because they have a specific skin problem that needs solving, they demand clinical proof that products will actually work rather than accepting marketing claims at face value.
Their specific dry skin needs make them attracted to customized solutions that can address their individual skin type rather than generic products.
Their need for proof extends to wanting to test products themselves before committing, using trial sizes as personal validation of claims.
They're willing to pay more but need scientific backing to justify the premium, making proof essential for higher-priced purchases.
Their desire to eliminate separate moisturizing steps makes them prioritize body washes that can handle both cleansing and moisturizing effectively.
Their laser focus on solving dry skin problems makes them quickly dismiss any benefits that don't contribute to this primary need.
Their dissatisfaction with current generic products drives them to seek more targeted, personalized solutions that might work better.
When products perfectly match their needs, they're willing to buy immediately, but for less relevant products they want trial sizes first, showing selective risk tolerance.
